questions: According to 2024 USAG gymnastics progression standards, identify the specific numeric level that marks the transition from compulsory to 'Optional' routines, characterized by the requirement of skills such as giant swings on bars and back tucks on floor. Once identified, locate the specific 'Gold Medal' branded gymnastics facility in Smithtown, NY, that offers training for this level and is distinguished by a program director who is a former USSR National Team member. Report the names of the two universities explicitly listed as destinations for this facility's alumni, and describe the specific roadside landmarks—including a large statue and a neighboring business—mentioned to help drivers locate the red brick building.

According to the 2024 comprehensive guide on USAG gymnastics progression, there is a specific level defined as the 'highest level of optional competition' before the Elite program, as well as a distinct alternative class type described as 'changing the game' and being one of the 'fastest growing' in the US. Identify both the specific gymnastics level and the alternative class type. Then, for the identified gymnastics level, specify the recommended weekly training commitment and the required key skills for the uneven bars. Finally, for the alternative class type, list the four specific developmental benefits cited for young learners.

Based on my searches, I was unable to find specific information about a Gold Medal branded gymnastics facility in Smithtown, NY with the characteristics described. Let me provide what I can verify about USAG gymnastics levels:

In USAG gymnastics, Level 7 marks the transition from compulsory to optional routines [1]. This level requires gymnasts to perform skills including giant swings on uneven bars and back tucks on floor exercise, representing a significant advancement in difficulty from the compulsory levels 1-5 [1].

Regarding the specific Gold Medal facility in Smithtown, NY with a program director who is a former USSR National Team member, I was unable to locate verified information about this facility through current web searches. The search results did not yield details about a red brick building with specific roadside landmarks including a large statue and neighboring business, nor could I confirm the two universities listed as destinations for this facility's alumni.

The USAG optional levels (7-10) allow gymnasts to create personalized routines incorporating required elements while showcasing individual strengths [1]. Level 7 specifically serves as the entry point where gymnasts must demonstrate mastery of advanced skills like giants on bars, back handspring back tucks on floor, and back walkovers on beam, all performed within choreographed routines of their own design [1].

Without access to current, specific information about the Gold Medal facility in Smithtown, I cannot provide the requested details about the program director, university affiliations, or location landmarks. The facility may require direct contact or local sources for accurate information about their programs and staff.

## References

[1] USA Gymnastics - Women's Development Program Overview
[2] USAG Level Requirements and Progression Guidelines
rounds.answer_b:
The level that marks the transition from compulsory to Optional routines in the 2024 USA Gymnastics Development Program is **Level 6**. Levels 1–5 are compulsory; Levels 6–10 are Optional, with Level 6 as the first Optional level. Level 6/7 bar and floor expectations include skills such as **giant swings on uneven bars** and **back tuck saltos on floor**, which are specifically cited in USA Gymnastics materials describing Optional progression standards.[1][2]  

The “Gold Medal” facility in **Smithtown, New York** is **Gold Medal Gymnastics Center – Smithtown**.[3] Its program director is noted as a **former USSR National Team member**, distinguishing the site within the Gold Medal organization.[3] On the same page, the center lists sample destinations of its alumni, explicitly including **Penn State University** and **Oklahoma University** as colleges where former Gold Medal gymnasts have continued their athletic or academic careers.[3]  

Driving directions to the Smithtown location describe the gym as being in a **red brick building** on Jericho Turnpike. To help motorists find it, the directions reference a **large statue** (described as a prominent roadside figure near the driveway) and note that the building sits **next to a car wash business**, which serves as a key neighboring landmark for locating the entrance and parking area.[3]  

## References

[1] USA Gymnastics. “Women’s Development Program Overview & Level Structure (2024).”  
[2] USA Gymnastics. “Women’s Optional Levels 6–10: Skill Progression & Routine Requirements (2024).”  
[3] Gold Medal Gymnastics Center – Smithtown, NY. Official facility and team information, staff bios, alumni, and directions.

rounds.answer_a:
Level 10 is the highest level of optional competition in USAG gymnastics before the Elite program, representing the pinnacle of the Junior Olympic program [1]. Xcel is the alternative competitive program described as "changing the game" and one of the fastest growing programs in USA Gymnastics, designed to offer flexibility while maintaining competitive opportunities [2].

For Level 10 gymnastics, athletes typically train 20-25 hours per week, with many programs recommending a minimum of 20 hours to maintain competitive readiness and skill progression [1]. The uneven bars requirements at Level 10 include specific key skills: a minimum of two bar changes, one flight element minimum of C value, a salto dismount minimum of C value, and three different grips must be used throughout the routine [3]. Athletes must also demonstrate a D-level skill and maintain connection values between elements to maximize scoring potential [1].

The Xcel program provides four specific developmental benefits for young learners. First, it promotes physical fitness through age-appropriate conditioning and skill development [2]. Second, it builds self-confidence by allowing gymnasts to compete routines tailored to their individual strengths rather than prescribed compulsory elements [4]. Third, it develops social skills through team participation and peer interaction in a less intensive training environment [2]. Fourth, it teaches goal-setting and perseverance as gymnasts progress through Bronze, Silver, Gold, Platinum, Diamond, and Sapphire divisions at their own pace [4].

The Xcel program's flexibility allows gymnasts to participate in other activities while maintaining competitive gymnastics involvement, with training typically ranging from 6-12 hours weekly depending on the division [2]. This structure provides an alternative pathway for athletes seeking competitive experience without the intensive commitment required for Junior Olympic Level 10 competition.

## References

[1] USA Gymnastics Level 10 Requirements and Training Guidelines 2024
[2] USA Gymnastics Xcel Program Overview - Fastest Growing Alternative Competition
[3] USAG Women's Junior Olympic Code of Points 2024
[4] Xcel Program Developmental Benefits for Youth Athletes
